title: Performance degradation when using ALIAS column with new JSON data type compared to direct JSON field access
description:
### Company or project name
_No response_
### Describe the situation
When using the new JSON data type, selecting a field via an ALIAS column produces significantly worse performance compared to selecting the same field directly from the JSON object.
### Which ClickHouse versions are affected?
25.11.2
### How to reproduce
```sql
CREATE TABLE default.json_test (
json JSON,
col_a Int64 ALIAS json.a
) ENGINE = MergeTree
ORDER BY tuple();
INSERT INTO default.json_test
SELECT formatRow('JSONEachRow',
number as a,
number / 10 as b,
number / 100 as c,
number / 1000 as d,
number / 10000 as e)
FROM numbers(10000000);
OPTIMIZE TABLE default.json_test FINAL;
```
Query 1 — Using ALIAS column
```sql
SELECT col_a FROM default.json_test FORMAT Null;
0.309 sec
```
Query 2 — Direct JSON field access
```sql
SELECT json.a AS col_a FROM default.json_test FORMAT Null;
0.047 sec
```
Its getting worse with bigger JSON
### Expected performance
Selecting an alias of a JSON field should have similar performance than accessing the JSON field directly.
### Additional context
Profile events query 1
```json
{
"Query":1,
"SelectQuery":1,
"InitialQuery":1,
"QueriesWithSubqueries":1,
"SelectQueriesWithSubqueries":1,
"FileOpen":28,
"ReadBufferFromFileDescriptorReadBytes":251771438,
"ReadCompressedBytes":248990021,
"CompressedReadBufferBlocks":7325,
"CompressedReadBufferBytes":482736197,
bug_features_seed: CREATE TABLE default.json_test_v2 (json JSON, col_b BIGINT, col_c VARCHAR(255));

INSERT INTO default.json_test_v2 SELECT number AS a, number * 2 AS b, CAST(number * 3 AS CHAR) AS c FROM (SELECT 0 AS number UNION ALL SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT 2 UNION ALL SELECT 3 UNION ALL SELECT 4 UNION ALL SELECT 5 UNION ALL SELECT 6 UNION ALL SELECT 7 UNION ALL SELECT 8 UNION ALL SELECT 9) AS numbers LIMIT 10000000;
SELECT col_b, col_c FROM default.json_test_v2 WHERE col_b IS NOT NULL;
SELECT json->>'b' AS direct_b, json->>'c' AS direct_c FROM default.json_test_v2 WHERE json->>'b' > '5000000';
SELECT col_b, COUNT(*) FROM default.json_test_v2 GROUP BY col_b HAVING col_b < 100;
key_bug_features: Using ALIAS column with new JSON data type
